title: Relations and Functions — Injective, Surjective, Bijective
sections: Visualisation — Injective, surjective, bijective at a glance
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Relations and Functions — Injective, Surjective, Bijective Intuition A relation is a connection between elements of two sets — like "student X is enrolled in course Y".  A function is a special relation where every input maps to exactly one output — like a Python function that always returns the same result for the same argument. Understanding the types of functions (one-to-one, onto, bijection) tells you whether information is lost, whether you can invert the mapping, and whether two sets have the same size. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 2: Set Theory (Cartesian products) From First Principles Relations A relation  from set  to set  is any subset of the Cartesian product . Pen & paper: , is a relation from  to . We write  or  to mean "1 is related to a". Properties of relations on a single set Let  be a relation on  (i.e., ). Let . Reflexive: Every element is related to itself. Example:  — reflexive ✓ Symmetric: If  is related to , then  is related to . Example:  — symmetric ✓ Transitive: If  and , then . Example:  — transitive ✓ Equivalence relation: reflexive + symmetric + transitive. "Equals", "same parity", "congruent modulo n" are all equivalence relations. Pen & paper: Is "congruent mod 3" on  an equivalence relation? - Reflexive:  ✓ (remainder of  is 0) - Symmetric:  ✓ - Transitive:  and  ✓ Equivalence classes:  — these partition the set. Functions A function  is a relation where every element of  maps to exactly one element of . -  is the domain (inputs) -  is the codomain (possible outputs) - The range (or image) is the set of outputs actually produced: Pen & paper:  defined by . - Domain = - Codomain = - Range =  (note:  is never hit) Injective (one-to-one) Different inputs always produce different outputs. Pen & paper: Is  injective? If , then , so . ✓ Injective. Pen & paper: Is  injective on ? , but . ✗ Not injective. (But  is injective if we restrict the domain to .) Injective = no information loss. You can always recover the input from the output. Surjective (onto) Every element of the codomain is hit by at least one input. Equivalently: range = codomain. Pen & paper: Is ,  surjective? For any , solve: . ✓ Surjective. Pen & paper: Is ,  surjective? Is there an  such that ?  No (in reals). ✗ Not surjective. (But if we change the codomain to , it becomes surjective.) Bijective (one-to-one and onto) A function that is both injective and surjective. Key property: A bijection has an inverse function . Pen & paper: , - Injective ✓ (shown above) - Surjective ✓ (shown above) - Therefore bijective ✓ - Inverse: - Verify:  ✓ Composition — apply  first, then . Pen & paper: , Order matters!  in general. Summary table Type  Each input →  Each output ←  Invertible? ----------------------------------------------- Injective  unique output  at most one input  left-invertible Surjective  some output  at least one input  right-invertible Bijective  unique output  exactly one input  fully invertible Counting argument If  is a bijection, then . This is how mathematicians prove two infinite sets have the "same size" — e.g.,  and  have the same cardinality because we can construct a bijection: Python Verification Visualisation — Injective, surjective, bijective at a glance The three classifications of a function — injective (one-to-one), surjective (onto), and bijective (both) — are easiest to see as arrow diagrams between two finite sets. Three terms with three real-world consequences: - Injective (one-to-one): no two inputs share an output. Hash functions aspire to be injective — collisions = security vulnerabilities (think MD5). - Surjective (onto): every codomain element is produced by some input. A surjection from "queries" → "rows of a table" means every row could be retrieved by some query. - Bijective (both): a function has an inverse iff it is bijective. Encryption is bijective — you have to be able to decrypt everything you encrypt. AES is a bijection on 128-bit blocks; if it weren't, ciphertext could not be recovered. Lossless compression is also bijective by construction. Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - Hash functions — ideally injective (no collisions), but in practice collisions happen - Encryption — must be bijective (so you can decrypt); AES is a bijection on 128-bit blocks - Database keys — primary key → row is a function; unique constraint = injective - Type conversions — int → float is injective; float → int is surjective (many-to-one via truncation) - Equivalence relations — "same hash bucket", "same connected component", "same equivalence class in union-find" - Game dev — mapping between world coordinates and screen coordinates is a function (projection) - Customer-to-account mapping (Business/CRM) — Salesforce and HubSpot enforce many-to-one functions from contacts to accounts; many-to-many relations (campaigns × leads) are stored as junction tables — relational schema design is applied function/relation theory. - Lookup tables in industry (Engineering) — automotive ECUs (Bosch, Continental) implement injective fuel/ignition maps; tax-bracket lookups in TurboTax/H&R Block are piecewise functions encoded as ordered relations. - Bijective database migrations (Operations) — when banks (Wells Fargo, HDFC) migrate accounts between systems, every old account ID must map bijectively to a new one — auditors verify injectivity (no duplicates) and surjectivity (no orphans). - Genome-protein mappings (Biology/Pharma) — the genetic code (DNA codon → amino acid) is a surjective non-injective function; pharma R&D at Roche/Moderna uses inverse mappings (protein → mRNA) to design vaccines and antibody drugs. Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Is the relation "a divides b" on  reflexive? Symmetric? Transitive? 2. Pen & paper: Is  from  to  injective? Surjective? Bijective? Find the inverse if it exists. 3. Pen & paper: Let  and . How many total functions  exist? How many are surjective? 4. Think about it: Why must a hash function with a fixed-size output (e.g., SHA-256 producing 256 bits) be non-injective when the input space is larger than ?

title: Combinatorics — Permutations, Combinations, Pigeonhole
sections: Visualisation — Pascal's triangle and combinatorial growth
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Combinatorics — Permutations, Combinations, Pigeonhole Intuition Combinatorics answers the question "how many ways?"  How many passwords are possible?  How many ways can you deal a poker hand?  How many routes through a graph?  It's the mathematics of counting, and it underpins probability, algorithm analysis, and cryptographic security. Prerequisites - Tier 0, Lesson 5: Primes, GCD (factorials use multiplication) - Tier 1, Lesson 2: Set Theory (subsets, Cartesian products) From First Principles The multiplication principle (rule of product) If task 1 has  outcomes and task 2 has  outcomes, and the tasks are independent, the total number of combined outcomes is . Pen & paper: A restaurant offers 3 starters and 4 mains. How many different meals (starter + main)? Pen & paper: How many 4-digit PINs (0000–9999)? The addition principle (rule of sum) If task 1 has  outcomes or task 2 has  outcomes (mutually exclusive), the total is . Pen & paper: A menu has 3 meat dishes or 2 vegetarian dishes. How many choices? Factorial -  (by convention — the empty product) - - Key values to memorise: ----------- 0  1 1  1 2  2 3  6 4  24 5  120 6  720 7  5,040 10  3,628,800 Permutations (order matters) A permutation is an arrangement where order matters. All items: How many ways to arrange  distinct objects? Pen & paper: How many ways to arrange the letters A, B, C? : ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA. Select  from : How many ways to choose and arrange  items from ? Pen & paper: Choose 2 letters from {A, B, C, D} in order. List them: AB, AC, AD, BA, BC, BD, CA, CB, CD, DA, DB, DC — exactly 12 ✓ Combinations (order doesn't matter) A combination is a selection where order doesn't matter. The notation  is read "n choose r". Pen & paper: Choose 2 letters from {A, B, C, D} (order irrelevant). List them: {A,B}, {A,C}, {A,D}, {B,C}, {B,D}, {C,D} — exactly 6 ✓ Why divide by ?  Each combination corresponds to  permutations (the different orderings of the same subset). Concrete example: The permutations AB and BA are different permutations but the same combination {A, B}.  For : each combination appears times in the permutation list.  For : the combination {A, B, C} appears as ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA — that's  times. So to go from permutations to combinations, divide out the repeated orderings: Key identities (pen & paper shortcuts) Why?  Choosing  items to include = choosing  items to exclude. This is Pascal's identity — the basis of Pascal's triangle. Pascal's triangle (pen & paper) Each entry = sum of the two entries above it. Row , position  gives . Binomial theorem Pen & paper: Permutations with repetition How many ways to arrange the letters in "MISSISSIPPI"? Total: 11 letters.  M: 1, I: 4, S: 4, P: 2. The pigeonhole principle If  items are placed in  containers and , at least one container holds more than one item. Simple but powerful. Pen & paper examples: 1. In a room of 13 people, at least 2 share a birth month.  (13 people, 12 months) 2. In any set of 5 integers, at least 2 have the same remainder mod 4.  (5 numbers, 4 remainders) Stronger form: If  items go into  containers, at least one container has  items. Python Verification Visualisation — Pascal's triangle and combinatorial growth Two of the most important pictures in combinatorics: Pascal's triangle (every binomial coefficient  in one geometric arrangement), and a comparison of how fast permutations and combinations grow as  increases — the foundation of the ,  complexity classes you meet in algorithms. Three combinatorial ideas everyone in CS uses: - Pascal's triangle = . It encodes the binomial expansion , the number of -element subsets of an -element set, and one row of the binomial distribution. Every diagonal is a recognisable sequence (1's, naturals, triangular numbers, tetrahedral numbers…). Adding adjacent entries gives the next row — that's the recurrence $\binom{n+1}{k} = \binom{n}{k} + \binom{n}{k-1}$. - The growth rates matter for algorithms. . Brute-force searching all subsets is  (intractable past ). Permutation searches are  (intractable past ). This is why TSP, SAT, and other NP-hard problems need cleverer-than-brute-force methods. - Counting = security. Password strength is a combinatorial argument:  passwords are not enough in 2025 (a GPU can try them in days).  is. Modern cryptographic key spaces () put a brute-force search past the heat death of the universe. Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - Password strength —  (lowercase only) vs  (all printable ASCII): combinatorics measures security - Algorithm complexity — "how many comparisons does sorting need?" is a counting argument () - Poker/game AI — probability of a hand = favourable combinations / total combinations - Machine learning —  appears in the binomial distribution (Tier 4) - Hash collisions — birthday problem: with  items and  slots, collision probability grows fast - Compiler optimisation — counting instruction orderings for pipeline scheduling - A/B test design & power analysis (Business) — companies like Netflix, Booking.com, and Google compute the number of variant combinations  when running multivariate experiments; sample-size formulas and lift detection rest on combinatorial counting. - Insurance & actuarial science (Finance) — Aon and Munich Re use combinatorial enumeration to count possible claim outcomes in mortality and catastrophe models; lottery game design (Powerball, EuroMillions) prices payouts using exact  counts. - Manufacturing tolerance stack-up (Engineering) — Toyota and Boeing use combinatorial enumeration of part-tolerance combinations to compute worst-case assembly fits and Six Sigma defect rates. - Drug combination trials (Pharma) — Pfizer and Merck oncology programs evaluate  regimens for combination therapy; FDA-IDE submissions for medical-device feature pairs rely on combinatorial design-of-experiments tables. Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: How many 8-character passwords using lowercase letters (a–z) and digits (0–9)? 2. Pen & paper: A committee of 3 must be chosen from 10 people.  How many ways? 3. Pen & paper: Expand  using the binomial theorem. 4. Pen & paper: How many distinct arrangements of the letters in "BANANA"? 5. Pigeonhole: Prove that in any group of 367 people, at least two share a birthday.

title: Graph Theory Basics — Vertices, Edges, Paths, Trees
sections: Visualisation — Drawing graphs (and their adjacency matrices)
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Graph Theory Basics — Vertices, Edges, Paths, Trees Intuition A graph is just dots (vertices) connected by lines (edges).  Your social network is a graph — people are vertices, friendships are edges.  A road map is a graph.  The internet is a graph.  Graphs are the most universal data structure in computer science, and understanding their mathematics unlocks shortest paths, network flow, and even Google's PageRank. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 2: Set Theory (sets, pairs) - Tier 1, Lesson 3: Relations (relations on sets) From First Principles Definition A graph  consists of: -  = a set of vertices (nodes) -  = a set of edges (connections), where each edge is a pair of vertices Pen & paper: Draw this graph: , Undirected vs directed - Undirected: edges have no direction.  .  Friendships. - Directed (digraph): edges have direction.  .  Twitter follows. Terminology Term  Definition  Our example ------------------------------ Adjacent  Two vertices connected by an edge  A and B are adjacent Degree   Number of edges touching   , Path  Sequence of vertices connected by edges  A → B → C → D Cycle  Path that starts and ends at same vertex  A → B → C → A Connected  Path exists between every pair  Our graph is connected Complete   Every vertex connected to every other   has  edges The handshaking lemma Why? Each edge contributes 1 to the degree of each of its two endpoints. Pen & paper: Our graph has edges , so . ✓ Corollary: The number of vertices with odd degree is always even. Representing graphs Adjacency matrix:  matrix where entry  if edge exists. Pen & paper: For our graph (A=0, B=1, C=2, D=3): Properties: - Symmetric for undirected graphs - Diagonal is 0 (no self-loops) - Row sum = degree of that vertex Adjacency list: Each vertex stores a list of its neighbours. Adjacency matrix  Adjacency list ---------------------------------- Space Edge lookup Best for  Dense graphs  Sparse graphs Special graphs Complete graph : Every pair connected. Bipartite graph: Vertices split into two groups; edges only between groups. Pen & paper: Is this graph bipartite? Colour 1→Red, 2→Blue, 3→Red, 4→Blue.  Every edge connects different colours. ✓ Bipartite. A graph is bipartite if and only if it has no odd-length cycles. Trees A tree is a connected graph with no cycles. Properties (all equivalent for a connected graph): - Connected and has  edges - Connected and removing any edge disconnects it - Any two vertices are connected by exactly one path Pen & paper: A tree on 5 vertices has exactly  edges. Traversals (pen & paper walkthrough) BFS (Breadth-First Search): Visit neighbours first, then their neighbours. Uses a queue. Starting from A in our tree: Queue: [A] → visit A, add B,C → [B, C] → visit B, add D → [C, D] → visit C, add E → [D, E] → visit D → [E] → visit E Order: A, B, C, D, E DFS (Depth-First Search): Go as deep as possible before backtracking. Uses a stack (or recursion). Starting from A: Stack: [A] → visit A, push C,B → [C, B] → pop B, push D → [C, D] → pop D → [C] → pop C, push E → [E] → pop E Order: A, B, D, C, E Euler and Hamilton paths Euler path: Visits every edge exactly once. Exists iff the graph has exactly 0 or 2 vertices of odd degree. Hamilton path: Visits every vertex exactly once. No simple condition exists (this is NP-complete to decide in general!). Python Verification Visualisation — Drawing graphs (and their adjacency matrices) Graphs have two equally useful representations: a picture (dots and lines) and an adjacency matrix. Drawing both side by side makes algorithms like BFS, shortest-paths, and PageRank much easier to reason about. Why both views are essential: - The picture is what humans use to spot bridges, cycles, central vertices, and disconnected components. Networking diagrams, flowcharts, and dependency graphs are all instances of this view. - The matrix is what computers prefer:  counts walks of length , BFS / Dijkstra / Floyd-Warshall iterate over rows, PageRank computes the dominant eigenvector. Spectral graph theory — community detection, graph neural networks — works exclusively on the matrix. - Sparse graphs need adjacency lists (storing only the existing edges) when the matrix would be 99%-zero. Most real-world graphs (social networks, web graphs) are extremely sparse — that's why Facebook's graph store and PageRank both use sparse representations. Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - Social networks — friend graphs, follower digraphs, community detection - Shortest path — Dijkstra's algorithm, Google Maps, network routing - Trees — file systems, DOM, syntax trees, decision trees in ML - PageRank — Google's algorithm models the web as a directed graph - Game AI — state space search (BFS/DFS), minimax on game trees - Neural networks — computation graphs; backpropagation traverses the graph in reverse - Knowledge graphs — entities as vertices, relationships as edges (used in NLP) - Supply chain & logistics (Operations) — UPS ORION, FedEx, and Amazon Last-Mile solve traveling-salesman variants on package-delivery graphs; Maersk and DHL use shortest-path algorithms to route container ships through ports worldwide. - Fraud detection (Finance) — Visa, Mastercard, and PayPal model transaction networks as graphs; cycle and community-detection algorithms (Louvain, label propagation) flag money-laundering rings — production tools include TigerGraph and Neo4j Bloom. - Power-grid analysis (Engineering) — utilities like National Grid and ERCOT model electricity networks as weighted graphs; minimum spanning trees and max-flow algorithms plan transmission upgrades and prevent cascading blackouts. - Drug-target & protein interaction networks (Pharma/Bio) — STRING-DB and Genentech model protein-protein interactions as graphs; centrality measures identify essential drug targets in cancer and infectious disease. Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Draw  (complete graph on 5 vertices).  How many edges?  What is the degree of each vertex? 2. Pen & paper: Given graph , , write the adjacency matrix.  Verify the handshaking lemma. 3. Pen & paper: Trace BFS and DFS starting from vertex 1 on the graph above.  Show the queue/stack at each step. 4. Pen & paper: Is the graph ,  bipartite?  Why or why not? 5. Think about it: Why does a tree on  vertices always have exactly  edges?  (Hint: what happens when you add a vertex to a tree?)

title: Proof Techniques — Induction, Contradiction, Contrapositive
sections: Visualisation — Induction as a row of dominos
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Proof Techniques — Induction, Contradiction, Contrapositive Intuition Proofs are how mathematicians (and computer scientists) know that something is always true — not just for the examples they tested, but for every possible input.  Testing tells you something works for 1000 cases; a proof tells you it works for all of them. You already use proof ideas when debugging: "If the bug were in function X, then Y would fail — but Y works, so the bug isn't in X."  That's proof by contradiction. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 1: Logic (implications, contrapositive) - Tier 0, Lesson 1: Number Systems (integers, naturals) From First Principles Direct proof To prove "": assume  is true, then use logical steps to show  must follow. Pen & paper: Prove "If  is even, then  is even." 1. Assume  is even. 2. Then  for some integer  (definition of even). 3. . 4. Since  is an integer,  where . 5. Therefore  is even. Proof by contrapositive Instead of proving , prove the logically equivalent . Recall from logic: Pen & paper: Prove "If  is odd, then  is odd." Contrapositive: "If  is even, then  is even." 1. Assume  is even, so . 2.  — even. 3. Contrapositive proved, so the original statement holds. When to use: When the direct approach is messy, but the contrapositive is clean.  Look at which direction gives you more to work with. Proof by contradiction To prove statement : assume , then derive something impossible (a contradiction). Pen & paper: Prove " is irrational." 1. Assume for contradiction that  is rational. 2. Then  where , , and  is in lowest terms (i.e., ). 3. Squaring: , so . 4. Therefore  is even, so  is even (proved above). Write . 5. Then . 6. Therefore  is even, so  is even. 7. But both  and  are even, contradicting . 8. The assumption was wrong.  is irrational. When to use: When you need to prove something doesn't exist or can't happen.  Assume it does, show things break. Proof by mathematical induction For proving statements about all natural numbers . The recipe (think of dominoes): 1. Base case: Prove the statement for  (push the first domino). 2. Inductive step: Assume the statement holds for  (inductive hypothesis). Prove it holds for  (each domino knocks the next one down). Pen & paper: Prove  for all . Base case (): LHS: RHS: ✓ Inductive step: Assume  holds for some  (inductive hypothesis). Prove it for : This is , which is the formula with . Another induction example Pen & paper: Prove  for all . Base case (): ✓ Inductive step: Assume  for some . We need , i.e., .  This holds since . ✓ Strong induction Instead of assuming the statement for just , assume it for all . Pen & paper: Prove every integer  has a prime factorisation. Base case (): 2 is prime — it is its own factorisation. ✓ Strong inductive step: Assume every integer  has a prime factorisation. Consider : - If  is prime, done. - If  is composite, then  where . - By the inductive hypothesis, both  and  have prime factorisations. - Their product gives a prime factorisation of . Proof by exhaustion (case analysis) Check every possible case.  Only practical when the cases are finite and small. Pen & paper: Prove  is even for all integers . Case 1:  is even →  has an even factor → even. ✓ Case 2:  is odd →  is even →  has an even factor → even. ✓ Disproof by counterexample To disprove a universal statement, you only need one counterexample. Claim: "All primes are odd." Counterexample:  is prime and even. Python Verification Visualisation — Induction as a row of dominos The clearest mental picture of induction: a row of dominos, each one ready to fall on the next. The base case knocks down the first domino; the inductive step says "if domino  falls, domino will fall too." Together → all of them fall. Below we use the same picture to visually verify the canonical $1 + 2 + \cdots + n = n(n+1)/2$ identity. Three big proof patterns in one place: - Induction = the domino chain. Used for every loop-invariant proof in algorithms, Peano-axiom-style theorems, and any statement with a natural-number parameter. Strong induction lets the inductive step assume all smaller cases at once — useful for recursion-tree analyses (mergesort, divide-and-conquer). - Geometric proof = "show by a picture". The right panel is a classic: two staircase triangles fit into an rectangle. Sometimes the picture is the proof — Bhāskara's Pythagorean theorem, the area-of-a-circle dissection, every visual-without-words you've seen on r/maths. - Counterexamples disprove. To kill "all primes are odd", produce — done, no induction needed. Fuzz-testing in software is the industrial version: generate random inputs to find a single case where the program does the wrong thing. Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - Loop invariants — proving a loop works correctly is essentially induction (base case = before loop; inductive step = one iteration preserves the invariant) - Algorithm correctness — merge sort, binary search, Dijkstra's are all proved correct by induction - Complexity proofs — "this problem can't be solved in less than " uses contradiction - Type systems — type safety proofs use structural induction over syntax trees - Cryptographic security — "breaking this cipher implies solving a known-hard problem" is contradiction - Testing vs proving — tests check examples; proofs (and formal verification) check all inputs - Formal verification in regulated industry (Engineering) — Airbus avionics, NASA flight software, and the seL4 microkernel are proven correct in Coq, Isabelle/HOL, or Lean before certification; aerospace standards (DO-178C Level A) mandate proof-based assurance. - Smart-contract audits (Finance/Crypto) — firms like Trail of Bits, ChainSecurity, and Certora use SMT-solver-backed inductive proofs to verify Ethereum/Solana DeFi contracts handling billions in TVL; Aave and Compound run formal verification before mainnet deploys. - Pharma & medical-device reliability (Industry) — FDA Class III device approvals (pacemakers, insulin pumps from Medtronic, Abbott) require induction-style safety arguments showing each firmware loop preserves life-critical invariants. - Cryptographic protocol proofs (Security) — TLS 1.3, Signal Protocol, and AWS s2n are accompanied by machine-checked proofs (Cryptol, EasyCrypt, ProVerif) that no adversary strategy violates secrecy/authenticity — the same techniques underpin DARPA HACMS-style high-assurance systems. Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Prove by direct proof: "The product of two odd numbers is odd." 2. Pen & paper: Prove by induction: . Show the base case and the full inductive step. 3. Pen & paper: Prove by contradiction: "There are infinitely many prime numbers." (Hint: assume there are finitely many primes . Consider the number .) 4. Pen & paper: Disprove: "For all ,  is prime." (Hint: try .)

title: Inclusion–Exclusion Principle
sections: Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Inclusion–Exclusion Principle Intuition If you count students who play football and students who play basketball separately, then add the totals, you have double-counted the ones who play both.  Inclusion–exclusion is the systematic fix: add the singles, subtract the pairs, add the triples, subtract the quadruples, and so on.  It is the counting law that turns Venn diagrams into formulas. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 2: Set Theory (unions, intersections,  notation) - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinatorics (factorial, ) From First Principles Two sets For any two finite sets  and : Pen & paper: In a class of 30, 18 take maths, 12 take physics, 7 take both. How many take at least one? How many take neither?  . Why subtract ?  When we add , every element in is counted twice — once in  and once in .  We over-counted it by 1, so we subtract it back. Three sets The pattern: + singles − pairs + triples. Pen & paper: A survey of 100 commuters asked which transport they used in the past week.  Bus: 50, Train: 40, Bike: 30.  Bus∩Train: 20, Bus∩Bike: 10, Train∩Bike: 8.  All three: 5.  How many used at least one? So 13 used none. Why add the triple back?  Elements in  are counted 3 times when we add singles, then subtracted 3 times (once for each pair), giving a net count of 0.  We must add them back once. General formula For  sets : In words: alternate addition and subtraction of intersections of size , for . Why does it work?  Pick any element  in the union, suppose it belongs to exactly  of the sets.  We must count it exactly once.  Inclusion– exclusion adds it  times, subtracts , adds , … and the binomial-theorem identity guarantees the net count is exactly 1 for every element. Application — counting integers divisible by ... How many integers from 1 to 100 are divisible by 2, 3, or 5? Let  = multiples of 2,  = multiples of 3,  = multiples of 5. - - - -  (divisible by lcm(2,3) = 6) - - - So 26 integers from 1 to 100 are divisible by none of 2, 3, 5. Derangements (a famous application) A derangement of  is a permutation with no fixed point — no element ends up in its original position. Hat-check problem:  people drop off hats, then everyone receives a random hat back.  How many ways does nobody get their own hat? Let  = permutations where person  gets their own hat.  We want permutations in none of the 's, i.e. . By inclusion–exclusion: Pen & paper: . Check by listing: of the 6 permutations of (1,2,3), only (2,3,1) and (3,1,2) have no fixed point. ✓ Striking limit:  as .  In a large room, the probability that nobody gets their own hat is roughly , independent of . Python Verification Visualisation — the alternating cancellation pattern The first plot shows why the alternating signs are needed: adding singles overshoots (103), subtracting pairs undershoots (71), adding the triple lands exactly on 74.  The second plot shows the eerie limit  — the chance that a random permutation has no fixed point converges to a universal constant that has nothing visibly to do with hats. Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - CS / Software — counting bug-free code paths, counting strings not matching any of  regex patterns, computing the number of integers coprime to  via  (an inclusion–exclusion identity in disguise). - AI / ML — multi-label classification metrics (precision/recall over overlapping label sets), data deduplication where records can match on multiple keys. - Databases — query optimisers estimate SELECT COUNT() WHERE A OR B OR C using inclusion–exclusion on column histograms. - Business / Marketing — customer-overlap analysis: "how many users opened at least one of three campaigns?" is a direct three-set inclusion–exclusion. Cohort analytics in tools like Mixpanel rely on this. - Operations / Logistics — shift-coverage planning: "how many days are covered if at least one of three on-call engineers is working?" - Probability — derangements give the answer to the matching problem, the lower-bound for the famous secretary/marriage problem, and the limit shows up in random-graph theory. Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: In a group of 60, 35 like coffee, 30 like tea, 25 like juice; 15 like coffee+tea, 10 like coffee+juice, 8 like tea+juice; 5 like all three.  How many like none? 2. Pen & paper: How many integers from 1 to 1000 are not divisible by 2, 3, 5, or 7? 3. Pen & paper: Compute  and  from the formula, then list every derangement of  to confirm . 4. Coding: Verify  to 6 decimal places by computing . 5. Insight: Why does inclusion–exclusion reduce to "+ − + −" rather than some other alternation?  Express the answer in terms of .

title: Stars and Bars — Counting Distributions
sections: Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Stars and Bars — Counting Distributions Intuition You have 10 identical cookies and 4 children.  How many ways can you give them out?  The trick is to draw the cookies as stars , and put bars between them to mark which cookies go to which child.  Every arrangement of stars and bars is one valid distribution; counting arrangements gives the answer.  The same trick counts integer solutions of . Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinations (the  formula) - Tier 1, Lesson 7: Inclusion–exclusion (used for the upper-bound variant) From First Principles The basic problem Distribute  identical items into  distinct boxes.  Each box can hold zero or more.  How many distributions? Picture: Lay out  stars in a row.  To split them among  boxes, insert  bars.  Stars left of the first bar go to box 1, stars between the first and second bar go to box 2, and so on. Example with  stars and  boxes (so  bars): The total number of symbols in the row is .  Of those, we choose which  are bars (or equivalently which  are stars).  Hence: Pen & paper: 10 cookies, 4 children → . Equivalent statement (integer equations) The same count answers: how many solutions in non-negative integers does have? Each  is just "the number of stars in box ".  Same formula: . Pen & paper: Number of  with  and .  Answer: . Variant — every box gets at least one If every box must hold at least one star, give each box one star up front. That uses  stars; the remaining  stars are distributed freely. Pen & paper: 10 cookies, 4 children, every child gets at least one → . Equivalent in equations: number of solutions of  with each . Variant — capped above (upper bounds) If  for each box, use inclusion–exclusion: let  = the set of solutions that violate the cap on box .  By stars-and-bars (with the substitution ) we can count , , etc., and subtract. Pen & paper: Solutions of  with for each. Total without caps: . Subtract solutions where : substitute , get , count .  Same for . Three single-cap violations to subtract: . Add back where two caps are violated (e.g.  and ): , count .  Three pairs: . Triple violation () needs , so 0. Final: . If items are distinguishable, the count is  (each item independently chooses one of  boxes).  Beware: beginners frequently mix the two. title: Pascal Identities — Hockey Stick, Vandermonde, Row Sums
sections: Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Pascal Identities — Hockey Stick, Vandermonde, Row Sums Intuition Pascal's triangle is a number tableau where every entry is a sum of two above it.  Hidden inside it are surprisingly powerful identities: a diagonal sums to a single entry below it (the hockey stick), two rows can be combined into a third (Vandermonde), and a whole row sums to .  Each identity is a counting argument disguised as algebra — once you see them, you stop computing from scratch and start seeing patterns. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinatorics — Pascal's triangle and Pascal's identity - Tier 1, Lesson 7: Inclusion–exclusion (used for the alternating-sum identity) From First Principles Pascal's identity (recap and proof) Combinatorial proof.  Choose  items from  items.  Pick a particular item, call it Alice.  Either Alice is in your selection (then choose the other from the remaining :  ways) or she isn't (then choose all  from the remaining :  ways). Add these mutually exclusive cases. This is the recurrence that builds Pascal's triangle row by row. Symmetry Combinatorial proof. Choosing the  items to include is the same act as choosing the  items to exclude. Row sum (subset count) Combinatorial proof.   counts subsets of size .  Sum over all  counts all subsets of an -element set.  Each element is independently in-or-out, giving . Pen & paper: .  Row 4 of Pascal: . ✓ Alternating row sum Algebraic proof.  Apply the binomial theorem to . Combinatorial proof.  Subsets of even size = subsets of odd size, when . (Pair each subset  with  for any fixed element ; this is a bijection between even and odd subsets.) Hockey stick identity Why "hockey stick"?  In Pascal's triangle, the terms on the left form the handle (a diagonal), and the answer on the right is the blade (the entry just below where the diagonal ends). C(2,2) + C(3,2) + C(4,2) + C(5,2) = 1 + 3 + 6 + 10 = 20 = C(6,3) ✓ Combinatorial proof.   counts -subsets of .  Group these by their largest element.  If the largest is , the remaining  items are chosen from in  ways.  Sum over . Vandermonde's identity Combinatorial proof.  Choose  people from a group with  men and women.  Either all  are women, or 1 man and  women, …, or all are men.  Sum these cases. Pen & paper:  from 4 men + 2 women. ✓ Two more useful identities Sum of squares: (A direct corollary of Vandermonde with  and , using symmetry.) Absorption (used for index manipulation): Proof. Both sides count committees of  people from , with one of them designated chair: pick the committee then the chair (LHS), or pick the chair then their committee (RHS). Python Verification Visualisation — diagonals and hockey-stick paths Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - CS / Algorithms — Pascal's identity is the recurrence powering the classic dynamic-programming solution for  (no factorials, no overflow); the hockey-stick gives closed forms for partial-sum tables in 2D DP. - AI / ML — Vandermonde's identity is the combinatorial backbone of the multivariate hypergeometric distribution (used in stratified sampling), and the binomial row sum  is the count of feature subsets in random forests / boosting. - Probability — the alternating row sum is the discrete sibling of inclusion–exclusion's parity argument; the absorption identity is the one-line trick behind the formula  for binomial random variables. - Compression / Information theory — the central binomial coefficient controls the rate of arithmetic coding for fair coins and is the count of monotone lattice paths used in Markov-chain mixing. - Business / Finance —  identities power American-option binomial-tree pricing models (Cox–Ross–Rubinstein); rebalancing volume in tree models depends on the hockey-stick partial sum. title: Recurrence-Based Counting — Tilings, Paths, Fibonacci

text: Recurrence-Based Counting — Tilings, Paths, Fibonacci Intuition Some counting problems resist a single closed-form formula but yield instantly to a recurrence: express the count  in terms of smaller counts .  Tile a  strip with squares and dominoes?  The answer is the Fibonacci numbers.  Count binary strings without two consecutive 1s?  Same Fibonacci.  Building the recurrence is half the skill; recognising the structure behind it is the other half. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinatorics (multiplication and addition principles) - Tier 1, Lesson 6: Proof techniques (induction) - Foundation 3: Sequences and series (recursive definitions) From First Principles A first recurrence — tile a  strip You have unlimited  squares and  dominoes.  How many ways  to tile a  strip? Set up by cases on the last tile. - If the last tile is a square (), the rest is a tiling of length :  ways. - If the last tile is a domino (), the rest is a tiling of length :  ways. These are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, so That's the Fibonacci recurrence (with shifted indexing): . Pen & paper: .  List them: SSS, SD, DS. ✓ . Recipe for setting up a counting recurrence 1. Define  precisely — what does the index  measure (length, size, step)? 2. Pick a "last move" — what was the last decision made?  The structure of that decision splits the problem into smaller subproblems. 3. Each case shrinks .  Sum the sub-counts (addition principle). 4. Find base cases — small  where the recurrence's "smaller problem" no longer makes sense. 5. Sanity-check by listing.  Compute  for small  both by recurrence and by exhaustive listing — they must match. Binary strings of length  with no two consecutive 1s Let  = the count.  Case on the last bit. - Last bit is 0: anything legal of length  before it.  ways. - Last bit is 1: previous bit must be 0; before that, anything legal of length .   ways. Same Fibonacci recurrence with different base cases — many counting problems are Fibonacci in disguise. Pen & paper: .  List: 0000, 0001, 0010, 0100, 0101, 1000, 1001, 1010 — eight ✓. Climbing stairs (the LeetCode classic) You can climb 1 or 2 stairs at a time.  How many ways to reach the top of an -stair staircase? Same recurrence: last step was 1 or 2.  . A non-Fibonacci recurrence — climb 1, 2, or 3 Now you can climb 1, 2, or 3 at a time.  Three "last move" cases: These are the tribonacci numbers.  Pen & paper: , . A two-variable recurrence — paths on a grid How many monotone paths in a grid go from  to , moving only right (R) or up (U)? Let  = the count.  Last move is R (came from ) or U (came from ): This recurrence's closed form is  (we'll see why in lesson 12). But for restricted grids (forbidden cells, blocked diagonals), only the recurrence works. Tiling a  board with dominoes Tiles are  dominoes (placed horizontally or vertically).  Let = number of tilings of a  board. Case on what touches the right edge. - A vertical domino: rest is . - Two horizontal dominoes stacked at the right: rest is . Same Fibonacci again.  , , . (For  tilings the recurrence is more complex — try it.) Solving linear recurrences (a peek) A recurrence  has solutions of the form where  are roots of the characteristic equation . For Fibonacci (): , roots and .  Closed form (Binet): We won't derive Binet here — it's the topic of generating functions in Tier 13. How many ways to climb 8 stairs?  Verify by listing for . 4. Pen & paper: A path goes from  to  with only R and U moves, but the cell  is blocked.  Use the grid recurrence to count valid paths. 5. title: Catalan Numbers — One Sequence, Many Problems
sections: Visualisation — five Dyck paths for n = 3
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Catalan Numbers — One Sequence, Many Problems Intuition How many ways to put parentheses around 4 numbers like so the multiplication order is well-defined?  How many monotone lattice paths from  to  that never cross above the diagonal?  How many ways to triangulate a convex polygon?  All have the same answer — the Catalan numbers .  When the same sequence appears across problems that look unrelated, it is signalling deep combinatorial structure. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinatorics (, Pascal's triangle) - Tier 1, Lesson 10: Recurrence-based counting From First Principles The Catalan numbers Closed form: Recurrence: (The recurrence is what you get from "case on the first matching pair of parentheses" — see derivation below.) Problem 1 — balanced parentheses How many strings of  pairs of parentheses are properly balanced? : () — 1 way. . : ()(), (()) — 2 ways. . : ()()(), ()(()), (())(), (()()), ((())) — 5 ways. . Recurrence proof. Take a balanced string of  pairs.  The first ( is matched by some ).  Inside that pair sit  pairs (for some ), and outside (after the matching )) sit  pairs.  Both inside and outside are themselves balanced.  Number of ways: . Sum over . Problem 2 — Dyck paths (lattice paths above the diagonal) Walk from  to  using only Right and Up steps, never crossing above the diagonal  (i.e. always have U ≤ R). : 5 such paths.  Same as . The bijection with parentheses: Right step ↔ (, Up step ↔ ).  "Never crossing above the diagonal" ↔ "never more close-parens than open-parens in any prefix" ↔ balanced. Problem 3 — full binary trees A full binary tree has every node with either 0 or 2 children.  How many full binary trees with  leaves (equivalently  internal nodes)? Pick the left subtree's size  and the right subtree's size : Same recurrence (with shifted index), so . Problem 4 — triangulations of a convex polygon How many ways to triangulate a convex polygon with  vertices using non-crossing diagonals? : triangle (3 vertices) — 1 way. : square (4 vertices) — 2 ways (two diagonals). : pentagon (5 vertices) — 5 ways. : hexagon (6 vertices) — 14 ways. Recurrence. Fix one edge.  Choose which vertex forms a triangle with that edge: this splits the polygon into two smaller polygons of sizes  and .  Sum gives the Catalan recurrence. Why does  count Dyck paths? The reflection (cycle lemma) trick.  Total paths from  to with R and U steps: .  We want to exclude bad paths that cross above the diagonal. Take any bad path.  At some moment it first touches .  Reflect the portion before that touch across .  This bijection sends bad paths from  to all paths from , i.e. paths needing  Ups and  Rights, of which there are . Good paths . Algebra check: Asymptotic growth Catalans grow like  — slightly slower than , much faster than any polynomial. title: Counting on Grids — Lattice Paths and Reflections
sections: Visualisation — paths through a forbidden cell, and the reflection bijection
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Counting on Grids — Lattice Paths and Reflections Intuition How many shortest routes are there from one corner of a city block to another if you can only walk east or north?  How many of those routes avoid a particular café?  How many never cross a river?  Lattice path counting turns geometric questions into combinatorial ones, and the reflection principle is the elegant trick that handles "forbidden line" constraints in one stroke. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinations - Tier 1, Lesson 7: Inclusion–exclusion - Tier 1, Lesson 10: Recurrence-based counting - Tier 1, Lesson 11: Catalan numbers (for the diagonal-reflection variant) From First Principles Unrestricted lattice paths A monotone lattice path from  to  takes only right (R) and up (U) unit steps.  The path uses exactly  R-steps and  U-steps, so it is determined by which  of the  positions are R's. Pen & paper: Paths from  to : . This is the most-used counting fact in basic combinatorics. Forbidden cells (one obstacle) Block a single cell, say .  Use complementary counting: count all paths and subtract those that go through . A path through  is a path to  followed by a path from to : So Pen & paper:  avoiding . Total: . Through : . Good: . Forbidden cells (multiple obstacles — inclusion–exclusion) If you must avoid two cells  and  (both reachable in this order, say  and ), use inclusion–exclusion: If the two forbidden cells are not in monotone order, you can pass through each independently but never both — drop the third term. The reflection principle (forbidden line) How many paths from  to  stay strictly below the line — equivalently, never touch ? Suppose  (otherwise the count is 0). Reflection trick.  A "bad" path is one that touches .  Take any bad path, find the first moment it touches , and reflect the portion of the path before that touch across the line .  The reflected start point is  reflected across , which is . This gives a bijection between: - bad paths from  to , and - all paths from  to , which need  R-steps and U-steps and number . So Diagonal Catalan case (): paths from  to  that never touch  (= weakly below the diagonal): That's the Catalan number from Lesson 11, derived purely by reflection. The ballot problem In an election, candidate A gets  votes and B gets  votes (with ). Votes are counted one at a time.  What is the probability A is always strictly ahead during the count? Think of vote orderings as lattice paths: each A-vote is a step "up" and each B-vote a step "right" (or vice versa).  By the same reflection argument, This is the Bertrand ballot theorem, the granddaddy of all reflection arguments. Walks with restricted moves — staircase counting Sometimes the allowed step set differs.  E.g. allowed steps are , , and  (diagonal).  The count satisfies with .  These are the Delannoy numbers.  No simple closed form, but the recurrence is direct. Pen & paper: .  Build the grid: So . title: Problem-Solving Heuristics — Casework, Complementary Counting, Symmetry
sections: Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Problem-Solving Heuristics — Casework, Complementary Counting, Symmetry Intuition Most counting problems don't yield to a single formula.  The art is in reducing them to problems you already know.  Three big reduction techniques: casework (split into mutually exclusive scenarios and add), complementary counting (count everything, subtract the bad), and symmetry (count one slice and multiply).  Each is a named tool.  Knowing which one to reach for is the difference between a 3-minute solution and a 3-page one. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinatorics (basics) - Tier 1, Lesson 7: Inclusion–exclusion - Tier 1, Lesson 12: Counting on grids From First Principles Heuristic 1 — Casework The idea: if the problem has different "shapes" of solution, split into disjoint cases by shape, count each, and add (rule of sum). When to reach for it: the problem mentions different "types" or "configurations"; the answer depends on a discrete choice (location of a maximum, parity, who wins a round, etc.); a slick formula doesn't seem to fit. Worked example — 3-digit numbers using digits  with no repeats and divisible by 5 A 3-digit number is divisible by 5 iff its last digit is 0 or 5.  Since 0 isn't in our set, the last digit must be 5.  Case on this immediately: - Last digit = 5.  First two digits are an ordered pair from  with no repeats: . Total: 12. The crucial step was recognising that "divisible by 5" forces a single case on the last digit.  The right case-split kills the problem. Worked example — colouring a 3x3 grid with 2 colours, no row entirely one colour Total colourings: .  Subtract colourings where some row is monochromatic. But wait — that's complementary counting (next).  The casework version: case on how many rows are monochromatic (), count each, and add. Often these two views coexist.  Whichever splits cleaner wins. Recipe 1. Find the "axis" along which the problem branches (last digit, position of max, parity, …). 2. Make sure cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive. 3. Count each case independently. 4. Add up.  Sanity-check: small instances by hand. Heuristic 2 — Complementary counting The idea: "at least one" and "no" problems are usually easier in reverse. When to reach for it: the problem says "at least one", "at most one", or forces you to track multiple disjoint sub-conditions; the complement is much cleaner ("no two adjacent…", "no element is fixed", "no row is empty"). Worked example — 5-digit codes with at least one even digit Codes from  with no constraint: . Codes with no even digit (all digits in ): . Codes with at least one even digit: . (Try the direct version — "at least one" — and you will end up with inclusion–exclusion over "digit  is even".  Same answer, much messier.) Worked example — number of permutations of  with at least one fixed point Total permutations: . Permutations with no fixed point (derangements):  (from Lesson 7). At least one fixed point: . Recipe 1. Read the question.  If it says "at least one X", ask: is "no X" easier? 2. Count the universe (typically a clean closed form like  or ). 3. Count the bad outcomes ("no X") — usually an easier subproblem. 4. Subtract. Heuristic 3 — Symmetry The idea: if the problem has symmetric structure (rotational, reflective, permutational), count one representative and multiply. When to reach for it: circular arrangements; symmetric polyhedra; problems where "rotations are considered the same"; problems where the answer must be divisible by some symmetry-group order. Worked example — circular arrangements How many ways to seat  people around a round table, where rotations are considered the same arrangement? Linear arrangements: .  Each circular arrangement appears  times in the list (one for each rotation).  By symmetry: If reflections are also considered the same (so a clockwise and counter-clockwise seating are equivalent), divide by 2 again: . Worked example — distinct necklaces with  coloured beads Naively  for  beads with  colours.  Quotient by rotation ( rotations) — but not all colourings have full -fold symmetry, so naive division is wrong.  The correct count uses Burnside's lemma (beyond this lesson).  The point is: when symmetry isn't uniform, naive symmetry-counting fails — but spotting the symmetry is still the first move. Worked example — count the number of different cube colourings under rotation Symmetry-aware counting tells you that 24 rotations of a cube reduce labellings of faces to a much smaller count.  Don't just divide; apply Burnside.  But the point is: see the symmetry first. Recipe 1. Identify the symmetry group of the configuration (rotations, reflections, label permutations). 2. If the symmetry acts freely (no configuration is fixed by a non-trivial symmetry), divide by the group's size. 3. Otherwise: think Burnside (out of scope here) or split into orbits. 4. Sanity-check: for small , list and group by orbit. Heuristic 4 — Bijection ("change of perspective") A meta-heuristic: if you can put your set into one-to-one correspondence with a set whose count you know, you're done.  Stars-and-bars (Lesson 8) and the reflection principle (Lesson 12) are bijection arguments.  When you find yourself stuck, ask: "does this set look like another counted set?" Quick example. Number of subsets of .  Bijection with binary strings of length  (element  is in the subset iff bit  is 1). Count: . Putting it together — a worked combined problem How many 5-letter strings from  have at least two A's and end in B? Step 1 — symmetry: the last position is fixed (B), so we're counting 4-letter strings from  with at least two A's. Step 2 — complementary counting: universe is .  Subtract those with fewer than 2 A's. - 0 A's: all from : . - Exactly 1 A: 4 positions for the A,  choices for the rest: . Bad: . Step 3 — answer: . (The casework alternative would split on exactly 2, 3, 4 A's: . ✓) Both work.  Pick whichever has fewer cases. Python Verification Visualisation — three heuristics, three diagrams Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - CS / Algorithms — casework is the meta-pattern in DP transitions ("case on the last action"); complementary counting is everywhere in probabilistic algorithm analysis ("with high probability X happens" = "1 − Pr[X fails]"); symmetry underlies group-theoretic algorithms in Burnside-based hashing. - AI / ML — complementary counting is the soul of the "log-sum-exp" trick and importance-weighted estimation; symmetry is the rationale behind equivariant networks (e.g. CNNs use translation symmetry; GNNs use permutation symmetry). - Probability — "P(at least one)" problems almost always solve via complementary counting; the birthday paradox, the coupon-collector bound, and the union bound all live here. - Cryptography — casework drives security-proof reductions; symmetry arguments under uniform distributions justify "the adversary has no advantage" claims. - Games — symmetry prunes game-tree search (chess opening books exploit reflective board symmetry); casework drives endgame-table generation. - Business / Operations — A/B test analysis of "did at least one variant beat control?" is a complementary-counting question; logistics scheduling uses casework on driver shifts and shipment classes. - Engineering — failure-tree analysis decomposes "system fails" into mutually exclusive cases (casework over component-failure subsets). Check Your Understanding 1. Casework: How many 4-digit numbers (1000–9999) have exactly two digits equal to 7?  Hint: case on the positions of the two 7's. 2. Complementary counting: A class of 30 sits at random in a row of 30 chairs.  Probability that at least one student sits in their assigned chair?  Hint: subtract the derangement probability. 3. Symmetry: How many distinct ways can 7 people sit at a round table (rotations equivalent)?  And if reflections are also equivalent? 4. Bijection: Show that the number of binary strings of length  with ones equals the number of -element subsets of . 5. Choose your weapon: A bag has 5 red, 4 blue, 3 green marbles.  How many ways to draw 6 marbles such that at least 2 are red?  Try both casework and complementary counting; report which has fewer cases.

title: Polyhedra and Euler's Formula — V − E + F = 2
sections: Visualisation — the 5 Platonic solids on one canvas
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Polyhedra and Euler's Formula — V − E + F = 2 Intuition Take any convex polyhedron — a cube, a tetrahedron, a soccer ball. Count its vertices , edges , and faces .  Then no matter which polyhedron you chose, the answer to  is always .  Euler discovered this in 1750 and it is the single most-quoted invariant in combinatorial geometry: it constrains what shapes can exist, proves the existence of exactly five Platonic solids, and underpins the entire field of topology. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 5: Graph theory (vertices, edges, planar drawings) - Tier 1, Lesson 6: Proof techniques (induction) From First Principles Statement For any connected, convex polyhedron with  vertices,  edges, and faces: The same formula holds for any connected planar graph drawn without edge crossings (with  counting the unbounded outer face). Pen & paper checks Polyhedron --------------- Tetrahedron        4   6   4    ✓ Cube               8   12  6    ✓ Octahedron         6   12  8    ✓ Dodecahedron       20  30  12   ✓ Icosahedron        12  30  20   ✓ Square pyramid     5   8   5    ✓ Triangular prism   6   9   5    ✓ The pattern survives any cut, any deformation that doesn't tear the surface. Why is it true? (sketch by induction) Project the polyhedron onto the plane (one face becomes the unbounded "outer" region) — you get a connected planar graph. Base case: a single vertex.   (just the outer face), so . Inductive step: add either an edge or a vertex+edge to a graph satisfying the formula. - Adding an edge between existing vertices either (a) creates a new face by splitting an existing face, increasing  and  each by 1, or (b) is forbidden if it crosses (we're keeping it planar).  Either way the alternating sum is preserved. - Adding a new vertex by attaching it to one existing vertex via one new edge increases  and  each by 1.  Net change: . So the alternating sum stays at 2 throughout. ∎ Consequence 1 — exactly five Platonic solids A Platonic solid is a convex polyhedron whose faces are all congruent regular -gons, with  faces meeting at each vertex. Sum of edges from face count: each face has  edges, each edge belongs to 2 faces, so . Sum of edges from vertex count: each vertex meets  edges, each edge has 2 endpoints, so . Substitute  and  into Euler: For a positive  we need  with (since faces have at least 3 sides and at least 3 faces meet at each vertex). The only integer pairs  satisfying  with : Solid ------------------------------------------ Tetrahedron     4    6    4 Cube            8    12   6 Octahedron      6    12   8 Dodecahedron    20   30   12 Icosahedron     12   30   20 That's it.  Five.  Forever.  Euler's formula plus a one-line inequality proves that no sixth Platonic solid can exist in three dimensions. Consequence 2 — every planar graph has a vertex of degree at most 5 Lemma: in any simple connected planar graph with : Proof. Each face is bounded by at least 3 edges; each edge belongs to 2 faces, so , i.e. .  Substitute into Euler: Corollary. In any planar graph, the average vertex degree is at most .  So some vertex has degree at most 5.  This is the key step of the 5-colour theorem for planar maps (and a precursor to the 4-colour theorem). Consequence 3 —  and  are not planar The complete graph  has , .  But . So  violates the planarity bound. ∎ For  a stronger argument is needed (using the bipartite property: every face has at least 4 edges, giving ). ∎ These two graphs are the obstructions to planarity (Kuratowski's theorem). title: Scalars, Vectors, Matrices, Tensors — Building the Hierarchy
sections: Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Scalars, Vectors, Matrices, Tensors — Building the Hierarchy Intuition A scalar is a single number.  A vector is a list of numbers.  A matrix is a grid of numbers.  A tensor is the generalisation to any number of dimensions. This hierarchy is the language of machine learning: inputs are vectors, weights are matrices, batches of images are tensors, and all computation is just multiplying and adding these objects. Prerequisites - Tier 0: Number Systems (real numbers) From First Principles Scalars (0-dimensional) A scalar is a single number: , , . In code: x = 5.  In physics: temperature, mass, speed. Vectors (1-dimensional) A vector is an ordered list of scalars. This is a vector in  (3-dimensional real space). Two interpretations: 1. A point in space: coordinates . 2. An arrow from the origin to that point: it has direction and magnitude. Notation: Bold lowercase , or with an arrow . Individual entries: . Dimension: The number of entries.   means  has  entries. Row vs column vector: Column (default): Row: Matrices (2-dimensional) A matrix is a rectangular grid of scalars arranged in rows and columns. This is a  matrix (2 rows, 3 columns).  We say . Notation: Bold uppercase .  Entry at row , column : . Pen & paper: In our matrix: , , . Special shapes: - Square matrix:  (same number of rows and columns) - Column vector:  matrix - Row vector:  matrix - Scalar:  matrix Tensors (n-dimensional) A tensor is the generalisation to any number of dimensions (called rank or order). Rank  Name  Shape example  Real-world example --------------------------------------------- 0  Scalar  —  temperature 1  Vector    a data point with  features 2  Matrix    a grayscale image 3  3D tensor    a colour image (3 channels × height × width) 4  4D tensor    a batch of colour images In ML frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow), everything is a tensor. Vector operations by hand Addition: Add corresponding entries. Scalar multiplication: Multiply every entry. Pen & paper: Linear combination Any vector in  can be written as a linear combination of and . These are the standard basis vectors  and . Matrix operations by hand Addition: Same shape required.  Add entry by entry. Scalar multiplication: Transpose: Swap rows and columns. A  matrix becomes . Python Verification Visualisation — Climbing the rank ladder Each new mathematical object adds one more index you can use to address its entries. Pictures make this very visible. The ladder makes the pattern obvious: each step up adds one more axis (one more index). A scalar has rank 0, a vector has rank 1, a matrix has rank 2, and so on. NumPy calls this .ndim; PyTorch and TensorFlow call it the same. A 4-D tensor (batch, channels, height, width) is the canonical shape for a mini-batch of images going through a CNN — and from this picture you can already see why: it's a stack of 3-D tensors (one per image in the batch). title: Vector Operations — Dot Product, Cross Product

text: Vector Operations — Dot Product, Cross Product Intuition The dot product tells you how much two vectors point in the same direction. The cross product (3D only) gives you a vector perpendicular to both. These two operations are the workhorse of computer graphics, physics, and machine learning. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 1: Scalars, Vectors, Matrices From First Principles Dot product (inner product) Given  and : The result is a scalar (not a vector). Pen & paper: Properties of dot product Property  Formula ------------------- Commutative Distributive Scalar factor Self-dot Vector magnitude (length / norm) Pen & paper: This is just the Pythagorean theorem! Unit vectors A unit vector has magnitude 1.  To normalise: Pen & paper: Normalise : Verify:  ✓ Geometric meaning of dot product where  is the angle between the vectors. Rearranged to find the angle: Pen & paper: Angle between  and : - - , - - Key insight for interpretation: -  → angle < 90° → vectors point "roughly same direction" -  → angle = 90° → perpendicular (orthogonal) -  → angle  90° → vectors point "roughly opposite" Projection The scalar projection of  onto : The vector projection: Pen & paper: Project  onto : This makes sense: the "shadow" of  onto the x-axis is . Cross product (3D only) The result is a vector perpendicular to both  and . Pen & paper: — the right-hand rule! Pen & paper: Properties: -  (area of the parallelogram) -  (anti-commutative!) - Memory aid: determinant formula Python Verification Visualisation — Vector arithmetic in 2-D Every operation in this lesson — addition, scaling, projection, the dot product — has a one-picture interpretation. Seeing the arrows once is worth a thousand component-wise calculations. Four take-aways for free: - Addition is geometric — just slide one arrow's tail to the other's tip; the resultant arrow is the sum. There is nothing "componentwise" about it; the components only fall out from this picture. - Scalar multiplication doesn't change a vector's direction — positive scalars stretch or shrink along the same line, negative scalars flip and stretch. - Dot product = projection length × magnitude. The green arrow's length is what most people intuitively think of as "how much of is in the direction of " — that's literally , and it falls right out of the formula. - Cross product outputs a new vector, perpendicular to both inputs (in 3-D only). This is why physics uses it for torque, angular momentum, magnetic force — anything where rotation around an axis matters. Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - Dot product — cosine similarity in recommendation engines and NLP embeddings - Orthogonality — independent features in ML, orthonormal bases in signal processing - Projection — shadow rendering, camera projection in 3D graphics - Cross product — surface normals for lighting in 3D rendering, torque in physics engines - The neuron — a neuron computes : the dot product is its core operation - Finance — factor models at AQR and Two Sigma — expected return on a stock is the dot product  of factor exposures (value, momentum, quality) with their premia, the basis of Fama-French and BARRA risk models. - Operations — Amazon "customers who bought" recommender — item-item cosine similarity (a normalized dot product) over purchase vectors powers Amazon's "Frequently bought together" widget, generating an estimated 35% of Amazon's revenue. - Engineering / Robotics — torque on robotic arms — the cross product  is computed every control cycle on Boston Dynamics' Atlas and KUKA industrial arms to balance loads. - Physics — Lorentz force in particle accelerators — CERN's LHC steers protons using , a cross product evaluated by superconducting magnet control systems thousands of times per second. title: Geometric Meaning of the Dot Product

text: Geometric Meaning of the Dot Product Intuition The dot product isn't just "multiply and add."  Geometrically, it measures how much one vector goes in the direction of another.  It's the mathematical heart of similarity, projection, and lighting in 3D graphics. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 2: Vector Operations (dot product formula) From First Principles The cosine formula (derivation) Start with two vectors  and  with angle  between them. The vector  forms the third side of a triangle. By the law of cosines: Now expand the left side using the dot product: Set the two expressions equal: Cancel  from both sides: What does the sign tell you? Condition  Angle  Meaning --------------------------- Same general direction Perpendicular (orthogonal) Opposite general direction Cosine similarity For unit vectors (): For any vectors: This ranges from  (opposite) to  (identical direction). Pen & paper: , - - , -  — very similar direction Orthogonality test Two vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular) if and only if: Pen & paper: Are  and  orthogonal? ✓ Yes, orthogonal. Projection (derived from the cosine formula) The scalar projection of  onto : The vector projection: Pen & paper: Project  onto : - - - The "shadow" of  on the x-axis is . Makes sense! The residual (the part of  not captured by the projection): This residual is orthogonal to :  ✓ Python Verification Visualisation — The dot product as "how aligned" The dot product collapses two vectors into a single number that tracks the angle between them. The plot below sweeps a vector around a fixed reference and shows how  moves through positive (aligned), zero (perpendicular), and negative (opposing). The single most important fact in linear algebra for ML: means the dot product is directly proportional to alignment: - +1 / large positive ⇒ vectors point the same way (aligned). In embedding search, this is "very similar". - 0 ⇒ perpendicular (orthogonal). In ML, "uncorrelated features". - −1 / large negative ⇒ pointing opposite directions. In NLP, antonyms appear as nearly-opposite embedding vectors. That single mapping — dot product → cosine of angle → similarity score — is the engine behind every embedding-based search system (Pinecone, FAISS, Weaviate), every recommendation engine that compares user/item vectors, every classifier head that scores classes against an embedding, and every attention head in a Transformer (where queries and keys are dot-producted to decide what to "look at"). Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry - Cosine similarity — the standard measure for comparing word embeddings, document vectors, and recommendation vectors - Lighting in 3D — Lambert's cosine law: brightness = surface normal · light direction - Orthogonality in ML — independent features, decorrelated representations, PCA - Attention mechanism — scaled dot-product attention in Transformers computes - Marketing / Business — customer segmentation at Spotify and Sephora — cosine similarity between customer feature vectors clusters users for the "Discover Weekly" playlist and Sephora's Beauty Insider personalisation. - Finance — beta and tracking error — a fund's beta to the S&P 500 is literally the projection of the fund's return vector onto the index return vector, the foundation of CAPM at firms like Vanguard and BlackRock. - Engineering — solar panel orientation — Tesla and SunPower trackers maximize  between the panel normal and Sun direction to extract up to 25% more energy. - Industry — semiconductor lithography alignment — ASML's EUV scanners compute dot products between measured and reference grating patterns to align wafers within 1 nm at TSMC and Intel fabs. title: Matrix Multiplication — Why Rows × Columns
sections: Visualisation — The three views of matrix multiplication
Connection to CS / Games / AI / Business / Industry
text: Matrix Multiplication — Why Rows × Columns Intuition Matrix multiplication is not "multiply entry by entry."  It's a series of dot products — each entry of the result is the dot product of a row from the first matrix with a column from the second.  This is because matrix multiplication represents composition of transformations: do one thing, then do another. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 2: Vector Operations (dot product) - Tier 2, Lesson 1: Scalars, Vectors, Matrices From First Principles The rule If  is  and  is , then: is an  matrix where: The inner dimensions must match: Pen & paper: 2×2 × 2×2 Compute each entry: Pen & paper: 2×3 × 3×2 Shape: Matrix × vector A matrix times a column vector is a special case: Shape:  — a vector. This is how neural networks work: Properties Property  Holds? ------------------ Associative   ✓ Distributive   ✓ NOT commutative   in general ✗ Pen & paper: Show non-commutativity ✓ Transpose of a product Note the reversed order. Column perspective is a linear combination of the columns of : Pen & paper: This is why the identity matrix does nothing — it just reconstructs the vector from the standard basis. Python Verification Visualisation — The three views of matrix multiplication The same operation  has three useful pictures, each useful in a different context. Seeing all three makes it impossible to forget the formula. Why three views? - Inner-product view (left) is the textbook formula — . Useful when you actually compute by hand or read the result entry-by-entry. - Column view (middle) says "matrix multiplication applies to every column of  in turn". This is what makes the identities  obvious: applying to  is the same as first applying  to , then applying  to the result. - Linear-combination view (right) says " is a weighted sum of the columns of ". This is the view used when reasoning about the column space of  — the set of all reachable outputs. Pick the view that matches the question. In neural networks the column view is most useful (each layer applies the same weight matrix to every column / token in a batch). In linear systems the linear-combination view is most useful (we ask "is  a combination of the columns of ?"). The textbook entry-wise formula is for debugging.
